content: Pulmonary embolism (PE) is obstruction of pulmonary artery or branches, usually by thrombus from deep veins (DVT). Annual incidence ~1-2 per 1000. Pathophysiology: thrombus dislodges from proximal leg/pelvic veins, travels to pulmonary circulation, causes mechanical obstruction (increased pulmonary vascular resistance, RV strain/failure) and humoral mediator release (hypoxic vasoconstriction, ventilation-perfusion mismatch). Virchow's triad: stasis (immobility, heart failure, AF), endothelial injury (surgery, trauma, catheters), hypercoagulability (cancer, pregnancy, OCPs, inherited thrombophilias). Risk stratification crucial for management: massive (high-risk) PE with hypotension/shock—highest mortality; submassive (intermediate-risk) with RV dysfunction but normal BP; low-risk without RV dysfunction or biomarker elevation. Clinical presentation: dyspnea (most common), pleuritic chest pain, cough, hemoptysis, syncope (suggests massive PE), tachycardia, tachypnea, hypoxia. Wells score and Geneva score estimate pretest probability.
